claimed_contribution: The study uses ambulatory eye-tracking in a real grocery store to directly measure consumer attention at the SKU level, beyond prior RFID path tracking or field-of-view video data.

Methodologically, it identifies lateral bias from exogenous variation in aisle traversal direction and vertical bias from exogenous variation in shopper eye-level positions, while controlling for shelf-level product quality differences.
It provides field-based estimates of the magnitude of lateral and vertical attention biases and shows how these estimates can be used to value shelf locations for retail shelf-management decisions.
context: The empirical setting is a large grocery store in a major metropolitan area in the Northeastern United States, with 109 product categories across produce, meat, center-of-store aisles, health and beauty, and checkout zones. The paper studies in-store attention during natural shopping trips.
design_and_data: The authors ran a 6-week in-store field study using Tobii Pro Glasses 2 ambulatory eye-tracking devices on intercepted shoppers who then shopped normally. After exclusions, the final sample is 175 shoppers, and manually coded video annotations yield 3,066 SKU-level attention incidences across 109 categories, with synchronized path, direction, shelf-height, and fixation information. For lateral bias, they estimate a fixed-effects logistic model leveraging variation in northward versus southward aisle traversal; for vertical bias, they estimate random utility models (with benchmarks and robustness checks) leveraging variation in shopper eye levels and controlling for shelf-level conditional purchase conversion. The paper also reports a survey of 43 retail professionals to benchmark practitioner beliefs.
focal_constructs: shelf height relative to eye level
main_findings: Shoppers have a higher propensity to attend to products on their right side while traversing aisles, with an estimated magnitude of about 21% when products are otherwise equivalent.

The data reject the idea that eye level is the attention-maximizing shelf position; the estimated ideal point is about 14.7 inches below eye level (around chest level, about 47 inches for the average shopper).
The vertical bias effect becomes stronger later in the shopping trip as shoppers have more items in their carts, while moderation by category hedonicity is not significant.
one_line_summary: Using ambulatory eye-tracking in a grocery store, the paper shows that shoppers attend more to the aisle's right side and that peak shelf-level attention occurs about 14.7 inches below eye level rather than at eye level.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior shopper-tracking and shelf-space research by moving from path or sales proxies to direct field measurement of SKU-level visual attention with ambulatory eye-tracking. It also challenges the practitioner rule of thumb that "eye level is buy level" by estimating an attention-maximizing point below eye level.
research_gap: Prior field research did not directly measure SKU-level in-store attention, typically relying on path tracking or downstream sales outcomes, so lateral and vertical attention biases were not directly identified in real stores. As a result, widely cited practitioner beliefs about right-side attention and eye-level placement had not been directly tested with field eye-tracking evidence.
research_question: Do grocery shoppers have a lateral attention bias toward one side of the aisle (right versus left) as they traverse aisles, and is this related to handedness? Do shoppers have a vertical attention bias across shelf heights, specifically whether eye level maximizes attention, and how this bias varies with category characteristics and trip stage?
